observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed that:
a) the Inventory Committee:
i. expedite the disposal of unserviceable properties in accordance with
the guidelines/policies stipulated in Sec. 79 of PD 1445 and COA-DBM Joint
Circular No. 2024-1 to deter further deterioration of such assets;
ii. initiate the transfer of ownership of PPE items that are already in
possession of and controlled by end-users other than the Municipality and
comply with the necessary documentary requirements. Alternatively, the LGU may execute a
Deed of Usufruct to formally authorize the concerned end-users to utilize the
properties while retaining ownership of the said properties;
iii. conduct an investigation on properties that are no longer physically
existing and prepare the necessary documentation to facilitate their proper
disposition, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006;
b) the Municipal Accountant:
iv. effect the necessary adjusting entries to address the noted
inconsistencies in the depreciation computation of the LGU’s
Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE);
v. effect the necessary journal entries to derecognize non-
existing/missing PPE items from the books, provided that all the
supporting documents required under COA Circular No. 2020-006
are duly complied with;
vi. ensure derecognition of PPE items from the books of account of the
Municipality once the transfer of ownership of other PPE items has
been completed, in compliance with IPSAS 17;
vii. ensure that the proper accounting and reporting procedures for all
acquisition/issuance/transfer/disposal of government assets are
pursuant to the existing manuals and other relevant laws, rules and
regulations after the completion of the one-time cleansing; and
c) the Municipal Accountant and General Service Officer:
viii. undertake collaborative procedures to ensure that all PPEs included in the
RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records and that the Property
Cards (PCs) maintained by the Property Unit and the PPELCs maintained by
the Accounting Unit are reconciled.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendations that:
1. the Local Chief Executive:
a) strictly demand from the accountable officers to expedite the liquidation of their
cash advances to avoid consequences of the penal clause provided by PD 1445
and COA Circular No. 97-002;
b) in coordination with the Accounting Unit and Treasurer’s Office, consider and
formulate a policy for the adoption of a cash advance system to facilitate the
granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances in accordance with
pertinent laws, rules, regulations and circulars;
2. the Municipal Accountant:
c) Intensify collection efforts by issuing demand letters to accountable officers to
liquidate or settle their remaining cash advances. This can be used as a basis to
implement administrative proceedings/sanctions to particular accountable
officers who failed to liquidate their cash advances despite successive issuance
of demand letters;
d) exhaust all remedies to demand the liquidation of the cash advance from the
retired employee and proceed with the procedures as provided under COA
Circular No. 2016-005 to request for write-off of the account, as deemed
applicable;
e) require all concerned accountable officers to submit immediately the
liquidation reports of the remaining unliquidated cash advances and submit the
same reports to the audit team for verification and to avoid the issuance of a
Notice of Suspension or Disallowance; and
f) enforce strictly the liquidation of the cash advances within the prescribed
period, in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002, Section 89 of PD 1445,
and COA Circular No. 2012-001 to prevent the further accumulation of the
unliquidated cash advances;
In addition, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and attach to each
cash advance voucher the required certification confirming that all previous cash
advances have been fully liquidated, in compliance with applicable regulations.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a) direct the concerned offices to coordinate closely with the respective source
agencies to obtain all necessary supporting documents, including but not limited to
Memoranda of Agreement (MOAs), Trust Agreements (TAs), and Fund Utilization
Reports (FURs), to establish the legal foundation, terms, and conditions governing
the receipt and intended use of the transferred funds;
b) in the absence of the required documentation, conduct a comprehensive
assessment of the status of the projects or programs funded by these transfers to
ascertain whether the funds were utilized in accordance with their intended
purposes;
c) after the assessment, engage the source agencies to determine the appropriate
disposition of any unexpended balances. If return is required, direct the Municipal
Treasurer to return/remit to the Source Agencies such remaining balance. Otherwise,
file a request to the Source Agencies for the authority to transfer the said unexpended
balances to the General Fund, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-001;
d) direct the Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the Bureau of the Treasury for
the remittance of the unutilized balance of the Priority Development Assistance
Fund (PDAF), in accordance with the cited Supreme Court decision;
e) furnish the audit team with a copy of the source documents (MOA, TA, or MOU)
of the fund transfers for review and evaluation; and
f) improve internal controls by implementing clear and strict procedures for
documenting, monitoring, and reporting fund transfers. This will ensure
compliance with financial standards, protect public funds, and prevent similar
issues from recurring.

We acknowledged the efforts undertaken by Management and further recommended that the evaluation of the remaining unremitted amount be expedited to facilitate the determination of appropriate corrective actions. Moreover, Management is urged to maintain full compliance with Section 5 of Revenue Regulations No. 17-2003 by properly withholding the requisite taxes on payment transactions and ensuring their timely remittance within the prescribed period.
observations.managementAction: Implemented The Municipal Accountant communicated and will evaluate the remaining unremitted account balances.

observations.recommendation: 1. For the Accounting Department to: a) Conduct periodic evaluation and assessment of the balances under the Due to NGAs Account to monitor its financial status, take up necessary adjusting entries, and to promptly address any issues on dormant/idle accounts; b) Confirm the balances with the Source Agencies to ensure the correctness of account balances presented in the financial statements; c) Properly classify and record the related transactions in its appropriate account titles (under corresponding subsidiary ledgers) to have a more reliable balances of accounts to be presented in the financial statements; 2. For the Local Chief Executive to: a) Require the implementing offices or departments to draw up the appropriate project proposals to utilize fully idle or dormant trust funds pursuant to the law governing said grant and/or the Memorandum of Agreement/contract with the respective source agencies. Exert efforts to properly coordinate with source agencies to secure copies of agreements previously entered into in relation to funds received. b) Require the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the implementing units, to determine if the specific projects, for which the funds were transferred, were already implemented. Ensure that liquidation reports were already submitted and received by the Source Agencies for proper adjustment in the books; c) Require the Municipal Accountant to verify with the Source Agencies to return or not the unexpended balance of the fund. If return is necessary, direct the Municipal Treasurer to return/remit to the Source Agencies such remaining balance. Otherwise, file a request to the Source Agencies for the authority to transfer the unexpended trust fund balance to the General Fund, as provided under COA Circular No. 2012-001; d) Direct the Municipal Treasurer to return the unused balance of PDAF in compliance to the cited Supreme Court decision by remitting it to the national treasury so that the funds can be utilized by the national government; e) Instruct the implementing department/offices to strictly monitor the implementation of the projects, including their timeliness and to facilitate the completion of the remaining on-going projects in accordance with the agreement, if any; f) Furnish COA with copies of the source documents (MOA, TA, or MOU) of the fund transfers for review and evaluation.
observations.reason: Not all balances were confirmed; Moreover, source documents such as Memorandum of Agreements (MOA), Trust Agreement (TA), Memorandum of Understanding (MOU), Fund Utilization Reports (FUR) and other similar documents were not presented; hence basis for verification and validation of the status of funded projects cannot be readily identified. There are unexpended remaining balances subject for return to source agency; Implementing units did not provide copies for the source documents.

observations.recommendation:
With the foregoing considerations, the audit team recommended to the Inventory
Committee, in coordination with the GSO and Accounting Department to:
1. Expedite the one-time cleansing of the PPE accounts in accordance with
COA Circular No. 2020-006;
2. Finalize and approve the Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) and submit the
same to the audit team at least ten (10) working days before the
scheduled start of the inventory taking activities;
3. Conduct the regular annual physical inventory of properties, if the one-
time cleansing will not commence on its expected schedule within the
calendar year, and provide for the necessary inventory reports to the
audit team;
4. Ensure that the proper accounting and reporting procedures for all
acquisition/issuance/transfer/disposal of government assets are pursuant
to the existing manuals and other relevant laws, rules and regulations
after the completion of the one-time cleansing.

For the Local Chief Executive to:
1. Create a sub-committee or group under the existing Inventory
Committee who will primarily handle the local road asset management;
2. Require the Inventory Committee, in coordination with the Municipal
Engineer’s Office to prepare and maintain an inventory of all Local
Roads. Likewise, the preparation of various inventory reports must be
observed yearly by the respective concerned departments;
3. Direct the Municipal Accountant to provide adjusting entries to
recognize changes upon completion of the required inventory reports
and reconciliation with the accounting records. Further, provide for the
necessary disclosures on the total road network system in the Notes to
Financial Statements.

Hence, we recommended for the management to exhaust all possible means to
document the legality of the ownership of the LGU over the parcels of land through
Transfer Certificates of Title (TCT) in the name of the municipality to avoid third-
party claims or any land disputes in the future. This will also ensure that the LGU has
the legal rights over the recognized assets presented in the financial statements.

With the foregoing considerations, we reiterated the following audit
recommendations:
For the Local Chief Executive to:
1. Demand from the accountable officers to expedite the liquidation of
their cash advances to avoid consequences of the imposition of penal
sanctions as indicated in CSC Resolution No. 040676, COA Circular
No. 97-002 and other pertinent rules and regulations; and
2. Institute appropriate administrative proceedings, if such cash advances
remained unliquidated after the prescribed period stated on the demand
letter.
For the Municipal Accountant to:
1. Require all accountable officers to liquidate cash advances within the
prescribed period in accordance to COA Circular No. 97-002 and
Section 89 of PD 1445; and
2. Issue demand letters to accountable officers with existing cash
advances not yet liquidated.

With the foregoing considerations, we recommended that the Local Chief
Executive, together with the Sangguniang Bayan and responsible departments to:
1. Cause the preparation of a justification on the deficiencies noted herein
and provide explanation on why the subject payment of Health Care and
Economic Assistance should not be disallowed in audit;
2. Strictly observe proper procedures in the appropriation, realignment and
utilization of funds:
a) Avoid lump-sum appropriations, except for those explicitly allowed
under the Budget Operations Manual for LGUs;
b) Utilize the MPBF only for deficiencies in authorized salaries,
bonuses, allowances, associated premiums and other similar
personnel benefits, as sanctioned by the President or other
authorized agencies;
c) Follow the rules on augmentation in which the source of savings
should come from each office’s respective appropriation and should
also be augmented to their respective offices; and
d) Set priority on the new and/or additional PS costs or reduce the
same to eliminate the excess appropriation over the computed PS
Limitation during the budget process.
3. Henceforth, refrain from granting any bonuses, allowances and benefits
to individuals in the absence of sufficient legal or statutory basis to
avoid audit suspensions/disallowances in the future.

Hence, the audit team recommended the following:
1. For the Local Chief Executive to:
a. Continue the priority programs, projects and activities of the municipality
for disaster risk reduction and to respond to the immediate needs of the
community during the pandemic;
b. Fast track the implementation of priority PPAs based on the scheduled
time frames indicated in the plan to further improve utilization rate of the
MDRRM Fund and to achieve the desired management outcomes.
2. For the LDRRM Council, in coordination with the Municipal Budget Office to:
a. Include the LDRRMF - STF in its annual LDRRMFIP in accordance with
COA Circular No. 2021-002;
b. Revise and update the AIP of LDRRM Fund in a yearly basis or when the
need arises; and
c. Evaluate the remaining unimplemented PPAs under LDRRM Fund, if
there is a need to realign or reprogram to more feasible projects.

1. For the Local Chief Executive to:
a. Continue the priority programs, projects and activities of the municipality to
support the attainment of socio-economic developmental goals and for the
general welfare of the constituents and to curtail the spread of COVID-19
virus through proper planning and efficient use of the 20% Development
Fund.
b. Fast track the implementation based on the scheduled time frames indicated in
the AIP to improve utilization and completion rate of the 20% Development
Fund and to achieve the desired management outcomes.
c. Consider implementation of projects “by Contract” since the management
does not have enough manpower to implement projects “by Administration”.
2. For the Municipal Development Council, in coordination with the Municipal
Planning and Development Office, to further evaluate the remaining
unimplemented projects under the 20% Development Fund which could be
realigned or augmented to other priority projects deemed necessary during the
pandemic, provided that the existing rules and regulations are being followed.
3. For the Municipal Engineer’s Office to fast track the preparation and drafting of
the program of works, project designs and other documentary requirements for
prompt implementation of various development projects.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and management agreed on the following:

a. Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare an adjusting entry to arrive
at the correct balances of accumulated depreciation and net book values.
Below are the suggested journal entries:
DR Prior Period Adjustments P9,108,648.86
CR Accumulated Depreciation1 P9,108,648.86
b. Update the accounting system and conduct a simulation test to check if the
depreciations provided by the said system is accurate. Conduct a random
manual computation on the depreciation accounts prior to financial
statement preparation to check if the balances are similar with the
generated data.
Management’s comment:
The management commented that all recommendations will be adhered to
however they explained that it would require more time to re-compute the items
of the PPE, including the fully depreciated items. The activity needs additional
manpower to give more time and focus on the recommendation. Moreover, they
conveyed that Municipal Accounting Office uses the manual operation in the
conversion of the old eNGAS to PPSAS since they have not purchased/subscribed
for a PPSAS ready tool to generate their Financial Reports.
They added that early in 2017, the management have assigned one
employee to do the reconciliation and monitoring of the physical count report
conducted by the General Services Office to fast track the identification of the
items to be dropped from the books. However, such employee was transferred to
the Treasury Office for a permanent position hence, reconciliation as well as the
computation of the depreciation for 2017 remained hanging and no adjustments
were made on the system. Verification for the accurateness of the depreciation
expense were made randomly on the generated depreciation expense of the old
system.

We recommended and management agreed on the following:

a. Maintain two separate schedules for Market and Slaughterhouse accounts
after properly determining the balances for each account.
b. As previously recommended in the preceding sub-topic of this AOM,
prepare adjusting entries to arrive at the correct balances of accumulated
depreciation and net book values. Below are the suggested journal entries:
Prior Period Adjustments P5,179,469.972
Accumulated Depreciation - Market P1,328,907.65
Accumulated Depreciation – Slaughterhouse 3,850,562.32
Management’s comment:
The management promised that all recommendations will be followed to
ensure that PPEs which belonged to the Slaughterhouse account will be separated
from the Market balances pursuant to PPSAS.

We recommended and management agreed on the following:

a. Create an Inventory Committee composed of two (2) or more permanent
employees including the supply/property officer or a custodian.
b. Conduct an immediate and thorough physical count of PPE. Prepare and
submit the RCPPE and IIRUP for the CY 2017 by following the formats
as provided in Annexes 49 and 51, Volume II, LGU-NGAS Manual.
Management’s comment:
The management commented that the recommendations are highly
appreciated and strongly noted. Actions will be undertaken to comply with the
above provisions.

We recommended the following:

a. Confirm the amount of the payables from the list of the identified creditors
to determine which of the payables are still valid and bonafide obligation;
b. Drop from the books of accounts the undocumented obligations /
payables;
c. Make necessary adjustments in the books of accounts if proven to be
without existing creditors for an accurate presentation of the payable
account in the Financial Statements.
Management’s comment:
The management commented that the identification, confirmation and
validation of the dormant payables will be done in the ensuing year and reclassify
the accounts if merited after exhaustion of best effort.

close the account and revert the same to the General Fund.

Municipality to make a resolution to close the account and revert the same to the
General Fund

We recommended and management agreed on the following:

a. Closely monitor the utilization and implementation of priority projects for
the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and
environmental management outcomes and to maximize the usage of the
fund;
b. Look for other laborers who could help fast track the implementation of
the developmental projects. If a particular labor is not performing as
expected, drop him from the roll of laborers and look for someone as his
replacement to ensure implementation of project as planned;
c. Ensure that program of works, project designs, coordination with other
agencies and other needed documents needing approval had been prepared
and approved without objections prior to its inclusion to the Annual
Investment Plan;
d. Fast track the implementation based on the scheduled time frames
indicated in the AIP by the implementing department concerned to achieve
the desired management outcomes; and
e. Strict compliance with the Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 of the
Department of Interior and Local Government and Department of Budget
and Management dated April 13, 2011 should be observed.
Management’s comment:
The management commented that the bulk of national and local funded
projects out-weigh the number of LGU personnel handling pre-implementation
and construction activities. Specifically, the lack of engineering personnel to
implement, supervise and prepare engineering design and program of work
contribute to the slow project implementation performance. With this dilemma,
the LGU initiated to pass and execute MDC resolutions No. 8, series of 2016,
Infrastructure Development Committee (IDC) Resolution No. 1, series of 2018
and Executive Order No. 15, series of 2017. However, these are not enough to
facilitate immediate implementation of all remaining unimplemented projects, but
the management is still willing to exhaust all other means to ensure completion of
all these projects as soon as possible.

We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Planning
and Development Office ensure the inclusion of all the on-going as well as all
projects that are to be implemented and are yet to be implemented for the
reporting year to warrant transparency and provide auditable information and
verification of other stakeholders of the government.
Management’s comment:
The management commented that the observation will be taken notably,
given that all data and documents necessary for the compliance of the said
circular can be made available to come with the prescribed report.
